symbols: group supporting a limp body — the Deposition from the cross restaged in a modern urban context
coyote and wild animals — death and wilderness encroaching upon the domestic world
birds circling — messengers between worlds, or carrion birds signifying death
suburban houses behind — the ordinary world where suffering occurs unnoticed
description: A contemporary painting showing a group of people on a sidewalk supporting a limp figure in flowing drapery in a pose clearly referencing classical Deposition/Lamentation compositions. The setting is a mundane suburban street with small houses, a utility pole, and a commercial building with a lit window. A coyote or wolf and a white dog approach from the left. Birds (crows or ravens) perch on the building and fly overhead. The figures wear a mix of contemporary and classical clothing. Graffiti-like drawings are visible on the building wall.
theological_description: Monkman's contemporary Deposition transplants the removal of Christ's body from the cross into a present-day Indigenous urban context. The classical composition — figures supporting a limp body in flowing drapery — unmistakably references the Deposition tradition of Caravaggio and Rubens. By placing this sacred scene on a suburban street corner, the artist makes visible the ongoing reality of Christ's identification with the suffering: 'Whatever you did for one of the least of these brothers and sisters of mine, you did for me' (Matthew 25:40). The coyote, a figure from Indigenous tradition, and the circling birds add layers of meaning about death, transformation, and the sacred within the ordinary. The mundane surroundings — houses, utility poles, parked cars — suggest that Golgotha is not only ancient Jerusalem but every place where innocent suffering occurs. Christ's death is perpetually present in the world's ongoing suffering: 'in my flesh I am filling up what is lacking in Christ's afflictions' (Colossians 1:24).

scripture_refs.reason: Whatever you did for the least of these you did for me — Christ present in contemporary suffering
The Deposition from the cross — the compositional and thematic source being reinterpreted
Filling up what is lacking in Christ's afflictions — ongoing suffering as participation in the Passion
